nodes.name: Great Composers
nodes.description: Major classical composers and their landmark works

items.prompt: Which Baroque composer wrote *The Well-Tempered Clavier*, the *Brandenburg Concertos*, and over 1,000 surviving works?
items.explanation:
German composer and musician (1685–1750), Baroque era
Wrote over 1,000 surviving compositions including cantatas, concertos, fugues, and chorales
*The Well-Tempered Clavier* (1722, 1742) demonstrated that keyboards could be tuned to play in all 24 major and minor keys
The *Brandenburg Concertos* (1721) are among the most celebrated orchestral works in Western music
Fathered 20 children; four sons (C.P.E., J.C., W.F., J.C.F.) became notable composers
Went blind in his final years after two failed eye surgeries by the same charlatan surgeon who later operated on Handel
Largely forgotten after death; revived by Mendelssohn's 1829 performance of the *St. Matthew Passion*

items.prompt: Which Austrian child prodigy composed over 600 works, including *The Marriage of Figaro* and *Requiem*, before dying at 35?
items.explanation:
Austrian composer (1756–1791), Classical era
Child prodigy who performed for European royalty from age 6; composed first symphony at age 8
Wrote 41 symphonies, 27 piano concertos, and major operas including *The Marriage of Figaro*, *Don Giovanni*, and *The Magic Flute*
His *Requiem* (K. 626) was left unfinished at his death; completed by his student Franz Xaver Süssmayr
Died at 35 in Vienna; cause of death remains debated (rheumatic fever is the leading theory)
Buried in a common grave in St. Marx Cemetery, Vienna — standard practice for his social class, not a sign of poverty
Rival with Antonio Salieri was exaggerated by the play and film *Amadeus* (1984)

items.prompt: Which composer wrote the *Ninth Symphony* containing 'Ode to Joy' and continued composing after losing his hearing?
items.explanation:
German composer and pianist (1770–1827), bridged Classical and Romantic eras
Began losing his hearing around age 28; was almost entirely deaf when he composed the *Ninth Symphony* (1824)
The *Ninth Symphony*'s 'Ode to Joy' melody was adopted as the Anthem of Europe in 1972
'Für Elise' (1810) is one of the most recognizable piano pieces ever written; the dedicatee's identity is debated
His *Third Symphony* ('Eroica') originally honored Napoleon; Beethoven scratched out the dedication when Napoleon crowned himself Emperor
Wrote 32 piano sonatas, including the 'Moonlight,' 'Pathétique,' and 'Appassionata'
Died in Vienna during a thunderstorm; an estimated 20,000 people attended his funeral procession

items.prompt: Which Polish-born composer, who lived most of his career in Paris, is considered the greatest composer for solo piano?
items.explanation:
Polish-French composer and pianist (1810–1849), Romantic era
Composed almost exclusively for solo piano — no symphonies, no operas, no purely orchestral works
Left Poland at age 20 and never returned; lived in Paris from 1831 until his death
Had a famous nine-year relationship with French novelist George Sand (Amantine Lucile Aurore Dupin)
Wrote 27 études, 21 nocturnes, 4 ballades, and 2 piano concertos, all still core repertoire
Died of tuberculosis at age 39; his heart was removed and preserved in a crystal jar in Warsaw's Holy Cross Church
His funeral at La Madeleine in Paris featured Mozart's *Requiem*, as he had requested

items.prompt: Which Russian composer wrote *Swan Lake*, *The Nutcracker*, and the *1812 Overture*?
items.explanation:
Russian composer (1840–1893), late Romantic era
Wrote three of the most-performed ballets in history: *Swan Lake* (1877), *The Sleeping Beauty* (1889), *The Nutcracker* (1892)
The *1812 Overture* (1880) features real cannon fire and is a staple of July 4th concerts in the U.S.
First Russian composer to gain lasting international recognition
His *Piano Concerto No. 1* (1875) was initially rejected by Nikolai Rubinstein as unplayable; now among the most-performed concertos
Died nine days after conducting the premiere of his *Sixth Symphony* ('Pathétique'); cause remains debated (cholera vs. suicide)
Corresponded for 14 years with patron Nadezhda von Meck, who financially supported him on the condition they never meet

items.prompt: Which German Romantic composer wrote four symphonies and the famous *Lullaby* (Wiegenlied), and was championed by Robert Schumann?
items.explanation:
German composer and pianist (1833–1897), Romantic era
One of the 'Three Bs' of classical music (with Bach and Beethoven) — a label coined by conductor Hans von Bülow
Took 21 years to complete his *First Symphony* (1876) under the shadow of Beethoven; it was dubbed 'Beethoven's Tenth'
Brahms's *Lullaby* ('Wiegenlied,' 1868) is one of the most recognized melodies in the world
Robert Schumann published an essay in 1853 hailing the 20-year-old Brahms as a genius; Brahms later grew close to Schumann's wife Clara
*A German Requiem* (1868) uses Luther's Bible rather than the Latin liturgy
Died of liver cancer in Vienna, eleven months after Clara Schumann's death in 1896

items.prompt: Which German composer created the four-opera *Ring* cycle and built a custom theater at Bayreuth for his works?
items.explanation:
German composer, conductor, theater director (1813–1883), Romantic era
The *Ring of the Nibelung* — four operas totaling ~15 hours — took 26 years to complete (1848–1874)
Built the Bayreuth Festspielhaus (1876) specifically for his operas; it is still used exclusively for his works each summer
Pioneered the 'Gesamtkunstwerk' (total work of art), integrating music, poetry, drama, and visual art
'Ride of the Valkyries' is one of the most recognizable pieces in classical music, famously used in *Apocalypse Now* (1979)
His music was later co-opted by the Nazi regime; Wagner's antisemitic writings and Hitler's admiration have shadowed his legacy
Influenced virtually every major composer after him, from Bruckner and Mahler to Debussy and Richard Strauss

items.prompt: Which Venetian priest and violinist composed *The Four Seasons*, among the most popular works in the Baroque repertoire?
items.explanation:
Italian Baroque composer, violinist, priest (1678–1741)
*The Four Seasons* (1725) — four violin concertos depicting spring, summer, autumn, winter — are among the most recorded classical works
Wrote over 500 concertos, 46 operas, and numerous sacred choral works
Nicknamed 'il Prete Rosso' (the Red Priest) for his red hair
Ordained as a priest but almost never said Mass, citing a chest ailment (possibly asthma)
Taught at the Ospedale della Pietà, an orphanage for girls in Venice, raising its orchestra to international fame
Died in poverty in Vienna and was buried in a pauper's grave; his music was largely forgotten until a revival in the early 20th century

items.prompt: Which German-born, British-naturalized composer wrote *Messiah*, including the 'Hallelujah' chorus?
items.explanation:
German-British composer (1685–1759), Baroque era — born the same year as Bach, about 80 miles away
*Messiah* (1741) was composed in roughly 24 days; the 'Hallelujah' chorus is the most famous choral passage in Western music
*Music for the Royal Fireworks* (1749) premiered in London with an audience of 12,000
Became a British citizen in 1727; composed the coronation anthem 'Zadok the Priest,' used at every British coronation since George II
Wrote over 40 operas and popularized Italian opera in England before turning to English-language oratorios
Went blind in his last years; operated on by the same surgeon (John Taylor) who treated Bach
Buried in Westminster Abbey; 3,000 people attended the funeral

items.prompt: Which Austrian composer, known as the 'Father of the Symphony,' wrote 104 numbered symphonies and mentored Mozart and Beethoven?
items.explanation:
Austrian composer (1732–1809), Classical era, known as the 'Father of the Symphony' and 'Father of the String Quartet'
Wrote 104 numbered symphonies, 68 string quartets, and numerous operas, masses, and concertos
Served the Esterházy family for nearly 30 years (1761–1790) as court musician
Mentored Mozart (they were close friends) and briefly taught Beethoven in Vienna
The 'Surprise' Symphony (No. 94) includes a sudden loud chord to wake drowsy audiences
Composed the melody for 'Gott erhalte Franz den Kaiser' (1797), later used as the German national anthem
His skull was stolen by phrenologists after burial and not reunited with his body until 1954

items.prompt: Which French composer wrote *Clair de lune* and *La mer*, pioneering musical Impressionism?
items.explanation:
French composer (1862–1918), Impressionist era
*Clair de lune* (1890, rev. 1905), the third movement of *Suite bergamasque*, is one of the most-performed piano pieces
*La mer* (1905) evoked the sea through orchestral color rather than traditional thematic development
Pioneered musical Impressionism alongside Ravel, though Debussy disliked the 'Impressionist' label
*Prélude à l'après-midi d'un faune* (1894) is sometimes called the start of modern music
Used the whole-tone scale, parallel chords, and non-Western tonalities (influenced by Javanese gamelan he heard at the 1889 Paris Exposition)
Died of colorectal cancer in Paris in March 1918, during German artillery bombardment of the city in World War I

items.prompt: Which Czech composer wrote the *New World Symphony* while directing the National Conservatory of Music in New York?
items.explanation:
Czech composer (1841–1904), late Romantic era
*Symphony No. 9* ('From the New World,' 1893) was composed during his tenure as director of the National Conservatory of Music in New York City (1892–1895)
Its second movement (Largo) became the song 'Goin' Home' and is often mistaken for a genuine spiritual
Advocated that American classical music should draw on African American and Native American melodies
Also known for his *Cello Concerto in B minor* (1895), which Brahms praised as near-perfect
Avid trainspotter who spent hours at Prague's railway stations memorizing locomotive numbers
Returned to Bohemia in 1895 and served as director of the Prague Conservatory until his death

items.prompt: Which Russian-born composer's ballet *The Rite of Spring* caused a riot at its 1913 Paris premiere?
items.explanation:
Russian-born composer (1882–1971), active in France and the U.S.
*The Rite of Spring* (1913) provoked a near-riot at its Paris premiere due to its savage rhythms and dissonant harmonies
Three early ballets for Diaghilev's Ballets Russes — *The Firebird* (1910), *Petrushka* (1911), *The Rite of Spring* (1913) — made him famous
Shifted from Russian nationalism to Neoclassicism (*Symphony of Psalms*, 1930) to serialism (*Agon*, 1957)
Became a French citizen in 1934 and a U.S. citizen in 1945; lived in Hollywood, California for his final decades
Buried on the cemetery island of San Michele in Venice, near Diaghilev's grave
Widely regarded as the most influential composer of the 20th century

items.prompt: Which Austrian composer wrote nine completed symphonies of massive scale and served as director of the Vienna Court Opera?
items.explanation:
Austrian composer and conductor (1860–1911), late Romantic era
Wrote nine completed symphonies (the unfinished *Tenth* was later reconstructed) of unprecedented length and orchestral forces
*Symphony No. 8* ('Symphony of a Thousand') requires over 1,000 performers
Directed the Vienna Court Opera (1897–1907) and the New York Philharmonic (1909–1911)
Converted from Judaism to Catholicism in 1897, largely to qualify for the Vienna Opera directorship (Jews were barred)
Died at 50 of bacterial endocarditis; his wife Alma later married architect Walter Gropius and then writer Franz Werfel
His music fell out of favor after his death and was banned by the Nazis; revived in the 1960s by conductors like Leonard Bernstein

items.prompt: Which Viennese composer wrote over 600 *Lieder* (art songs), including 'Erl King,' and died at just 31?
items.explanation:
Austrian composer (1797–1828), bridged Classical and Romantic eras
Wrote over 600 Lieder (art songs), more than any other major composer — including 'Erlkönig' (1815), composed at age 18
His *'Unfinished' Symphony* (No. 8, 1822) has only two movements; why he stopped is unknown
Composed the song cycle *Winterreise* (1827), one of the pinnacles of the art-song repertoire
Lived in poverty in Vienna and never held a permanent musical post despite prolific output
Died at 31, likely of typhoid fever, though syphilis and mercury poisoning (a treatment for it) contributed
Much of his work was unpublished at death; the 'Great' C major Symphony (No. 9) was not performed until Schumann championed it in 1839
